title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Bullying in the Workplace
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy helps survivors of workplace bullying heal their nervous system and reclaim safety and agency.
body:
Understanding Workplace Bullying as Trauma

Workplace bullying is more than just unpleasant interactions; it can be a form of chronic trauma that deeply affects the nervous system. Unlike a single traumatic event, bullying is often repeated over time, eroding a person’s sense of safety, control, and self-worth. Bessel van der Kolk, a leading trauma researcher, emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body as well as the mind, and the persistent stress of bullying can lead to dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system (ANS) that governs our fight, flight, and freeze responses.

Survivors often report physical symptoms—like muscle tension, headaches, or digestive issues—that traditional talk therapy may not fully address. This is where somatic therapy comes in: it works directly with the body’s responses to trauma, helping survivors rebuild resilience and restore a sense of safety from the inside out.

Why Somatic Therapy Works for Workplace Bullying Survivors

Somatic therapy is grounded in the understanding that trauma is held in the body’s implicit memory, influencing emotional and physiological reactions even when we're unaware of the triggers. Pioneers like Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, and Pat Ogden, who developed Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, have shown that engaging the body in trauma healing can unlock stuck energy and promote nervous system regulation.

In the context of workplace bullying, where verbal and emotional abuse creates a chronic state of threat, somatic therapy helps survivors:

  • Recognize and track bodily sensations linked to stress and fear
  • Restore autonomic nervous system balance using regulation techniques
  • Develop somatic resources—physical and sensory anchors—to regain presence and agency
  • Release immobilization responses such as freeze or collapse that often accompany chronic bullying

The Neuroscience Behind Somatic Healing

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a useful framework for understanding how the nervous system responds to social threat. When bullied, the social engagement system, which helps us feel safe and connected, shuts down, triggering defensive states like fight/flight or shutdown. This can leave survivors feeling isolated, hypervigilant, or numb.

Somatic therapy uses techniques to gently activate the ventral vagal complex—the part of the nervous system associated with safety and social connection—enabling survivors to move out of survival mode. Deb Dana, a leading clinician and author on Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes how body-based awareness and regulation exercises can help survivors re-establish nervous system balance and feel safe both internally and relationally.

Common Somatic Techniques Used With Bullying Survivors

Somatic therapists employ a variety of body-centered practices tailored to each individual’s needs. Some of the most effective include:

  • Body Scan and Breath Awareness: Bringing mindful attention to areas of tension or discomfort to increase interoceptive awareness.
  • Grounding and Orientation: Techniques that help survivors reorient to the present moment and their physical environment, counteracting dissociation.
  • Movement and Gestural Expression: Using subtle or intentional movements to discharge trapped energy and shift nervous system states.
  • Tracking Sensations: Learning to identify and follow the ebb and flow of bodily sensations linked to traumatic memories or triggers.
  • Resourcing: Cultivating internal and external somatic resources such as feelings of safety, strength, or support.

How Somatic Therapy Complements Traditional Talk Therapy

Many survivors come to therapy well versed in cognitive strategies but still feel stuck in their body’s reaction patterns. Somatic therapy does not replace talk therapy; rather, it complements it by addressing trauma at a level talk alone cannot reach.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model, for example, can be integrated with somatic work to help survivors identify and soothe vulnerable parts activated by bullying, while simultaneously calming the body’s physiological response. Francine Shapiro’s EMDR also incorporates somatic elements by using bilateral stimulation to process trauma stored in the nervous system.

By combining verbal and somatic approaches, survivors can achieve deeper healing, gaining both insight and a restored sense of bodily safety.

Practical Self-Directed Somatic Exercises for Survivors

For those not currently in therapy or looking to supplement their healing, simple somatic exercises can make a difference. Here are some foundational practices inspired by experts like Gabor Maté and Dan Siegel:

  • Mindful Breath: Practice slow, deep diaphragmatic breathing to engage the parasympathetic nervous system and calm hyperarousal.
  • Body Awareness Check-Ins: Pause periodically to notice any tension or discomfort without judgment.
  • Safe Place Visualization: Imagine a place or feeling that brings comfort and safety, focusing on sensory details.
  • Grounding: Press your feet firmly into the floor or feel your body supported by your chair to reconnect with the present.
  • Gentle Movement: Stretch or sway gently to help release stuck energy and refresh the nervous system.

Challenges Survivors May Face in Somatic Therapy

While somatic therapy offers powerful tools, survivors of workplace bullying might encounter specific challenges:

  • Difficulty Accessing Sensations: Chronic stress and dissociation may numb bodily awareness initially.
  • Overwhelm: Early somatic work can sometimes trigger intense emotions or sensations, requiring careful pacing.
  • Trust Issues: Survivors who have experienced betrayal may struggle to trust their own body or the therapist.

Experienced somatic therapists work with these challenges by creating a safe, paced environment and helping survivors build resilience gradually.

"Trauma is not just an event that took place sometime in the past; it is also the imprint left by that experience on mind, brain, and body." – Bessel van der Kolk

Finding the Right Somatic Therapist

When seeking somatic therapy for workplace bullying trauma, look for practitioners with specialized training in trauma and somatic approaches such as Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal-informed therapy. A skilled therapist will prioritize establishing safety, attunement, and collaboration.

Remember, healing from workplace bullying trauma is a process that takes time. Somatic therapy offers a path to reconnect with your body, regulate your nervous system, and reclaim your sense of self beyond the shadow of bullying.

By addressing trauma where it lives—in your body—you can begin to move from survival to thriving.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for bullying trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body's responses and nervous system regulation, addressing trauma stored in the body, whereas traditional talk therapy primarily works with cognitive processing and verbal expression.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help with physical symptoms caused by workplace bullying?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic therapy can alleviate physical symptoms like muscle tension, headaches, or digestive issues by calming the nervous system and releasing trapped stress.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy safe for people who feel disconnected from their bodies?
faqs.a: When guided by a trained therapist, somatic therapy is designed to be paced and supportive, helping individuals gradually increase bodily awareness without overwhelm.

faqs.q: What self-help somatic techniques can I practice if I’m not in therapy?
faqs.a: Simple exercises like mindful breathing, grounding, body scans, and gentle movement can help regulate your nervous system and build resilience in daily life.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the body and mind, offering science-based insights into somatic healing.
